---
title: "Chapter 2: Regional MPM pipelines"
subtitle: "Mineral prospectivity on public open data: four regions, four deposit types"
---

[← back to portfolio map](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/)

## What this chapter is about

This chapter walks through four regional pipelines I built: Eastern
Alaska (porphyry Cu-Mo), the BC Golden Triangle (multi-deposit), the
California Mother Lode (orogenic Au), and the Arizona porphyry-Cu
belt. The stack is the same in each one: USGS, NRCan, and
state-survey open data; feature-frame assembly with
`build_feature_frame()`; Random Forest classification with 20-km
spatial-block cross-validation; leak-free out-of-fold scoring; and
bootstrap confidence intervals on AUC and top-k% capture rates. Each
region is its own geologically distinct problem and tests whether
the approach holds up.

![Tanacross (eastern Alaska) porphyry-Cu prospectivity: random forest output across the bounded study area. Yellow cells are the highest-ranked; red crosses are the 56 ARDF porphyry positives held out for evaluation.](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/data/derived/eastak/fig_prospectivity_tanacross.png)

## Headline results

| Region | Commodity | Positives | Top-1% capture | Top-5% capture | Spatial-block CV AUC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Eastern Alaska v1 (Tanacross + Mt Hayes + Nabesna) | porphyry Cu-Mo | 45 | 22.2% (22×) | 64% (13×) | n/a |
| BC Golden Triangle v2 (multi-deposit) | Cu / Au / Ag / Zn | 154 (blind set) | n/a | n/a | n/a |
| Mother Lode v3 / v3.1 (Sierra foothills) | orogenic Au | 6,149 | n/a | 19.5% (4×) | 0.85 [0.79, 0.90] |
| Arizona v4 (SE porphyry belt) | porphyry Cu | 191 | 23.0% (23×) | 51.3% (10×) | n/a |
| Northern Sierra placer v6 (two-population fusion) | placer Au | TBD (Hydraulic-Pit + MRDS) | TBD | TBD | gldbg-handoff raster |

Top-1% and top-5% are capture rates: fraction of held-out positives
in the top k% of cells the model ranks most prospective. The
parenthetical ×N is the enrichment factor over what a random sample
would produce. The 22× enrichment on Tanacross and the 23×
enrichment on Arizona sit in the same regime across two porphyry-Cu
provinces 4,000 km apart. That's the central reproducibility claim
the four-region work supports.

## How the pipeline works

Federal and state agencies publish decades of geophysical,
geochemical, and geological data on national grids. My pipeline:

1. **Fetch** raw rasters and shapefiles from public APIs: USGS
   aeromagnetic and Bouguer/isostatic gravity, USGS State Geologic
   Map Compilation (SGMC) lithology and structural faults, USGS
   Mineral Resources Data System (MRDS) occurrence records, USGS
   National Geochemical Database (NGDB) stream-sediment chemistry,
   USGS 30-m DEM, Sentinel-2 SWIR alteration indices, plus
   region-specific feeds (Alaska Geochemical Database AGDB4 v4 for
   Tanacross; British Columbia Geological Survey RGS/MINFILE for
   BCGT).
2. **Reproject** every layer onto a common equal-area CRS
   (California Albers EPSG:3310 for the CONUS work; NAD83/Alaska
   Albers for Tanacross). Resample onto a 500-m grid.
3. **Assemble** the per-cell feature vector: geophysics columns,
   four magnetic-field derivatives (1VD, HGM, analytic signal,
   tilt), pathfinder geochemistry by element (5-km radius mean,
   max, count, has-data flag), SGMC top-level lithology and
   MAJOR1/2/3 fine-grained lithology, distance-to-fault, Sentinel-2
   indices, slope, aspect, and terrain ruggedness from the DEM.
4. **Clean labels** via Cox-Singer-aware filtering of the MRDS
   free-text deposit-type field. For Mother Lode this drops placer,
   epithermal, and skarn-Au records and keeps in-place orogenic-Au;
   for Tanacross it keeps porphyry-Cu plus distal-porphyry; for
   Arizona it keeps porphyry-Cu while excluding porphyry-Mo and
   skarn-Cu (tracked for diagnostics).
5. **Train** Random Forest with `class_weight='balanced_subsample'`,
   pseudo-negatives sampled with a 5-km exclusion zone around any
   known occurrence.
6. **Score** with 20-km spatial-block CV. Every cell is predicted
   by a model that did not train on its block. Bootstrap CIs on AUC
   and top-k% capture.

## The Mother Lode v3.1 increment

Mother Lode shipped as v3 in April 2026 with an AUC of 0.85 under
spatial-block CV, then iterated through eight v3.1 experiments that
either improved or tightened the result:

- **B1.** Magnetic-field derivatives (1VD, HGM, analytic signal,
  tilt) added. About 1 pp AUC gain.
- **B2.** SGMC MAJOR1/2/3 fine-grained lithology one-hots
  factorized and encoded. Modest gain, mostly improves SHAP
  feature-attribution interpretability.
- **C1.** Weighted-PU port from Hajihosseinlou et al. 2025 with
  Optuna TPE search on the weight ratio. Implemented and scored
  alongside the bagging-PU baseline.
- **C2.** Geology-only model plus Klamath cross-region transfer.
  Klamath AUC 0.58, barely above random. Documented as a known
  limit, not hidden.
- **D1.** MRDS Cox-Singer-cleaned positive labels dropped 20% of
  the v3 positives that were placer, epithermal, or skarn-Au
  rather than in-place orogenic. AUC roughly unchanged; the cleanup
  is for correctness, not performance.

## What the four regions together show

**The methodology reproduces across deposit type and region.** Top-1%
capture of 22% on Tanacross (porphyry-Cu) and 23% on Arizona
(porphyry-Cu, different continental crust setting) lands in the
same regime. The pipeline is portable.

**Cross-region transfer is the unsolved frontier.** Mother Lode →
Klamath (geology-only): AUC 0.58. Eastern Alaska v1 blind test
against Kenorland's 2023 porphyry intersection scored at the 62nd
percentile of the model's prospectivity ranking; the diagnostic
traced this to exploration bias in the training labels. Four
documented remediations did not rescue the blind test. The fix
needs methodology-level changes. See
[Chapter 4 audits](reproductions.qmd) for how the published
literature deals with the same problem (it mostly doesn't).

**SHAP feature attribution matches geology.** On Mother Lode the
top features are distance-to-greenstone-belt, distance-to-fault,
NGDB arsenic and antimony, plus the Calaveras Complex and Mariposa
Slate lithology one-hots, which is the canonical orogenic-Au
recipe. On Arizona the top features are AGDB4 Cu and Mo
stream-sediment, porphyry-host intrusive lithology, and
distance-to-fault, which is the canonical porphyry-Cu recipe.
Reasonable model behavior.

## Northern Sierra placer v6 (two-population fusion)

Placer prospectivity sits about a decade behind lode in published
methods; only the USGS Alaska watershed scorer (Cross, Frost, Karl)
survives as a production-grade transferable model. The northern Sierra
deep-gravel belt is the canonical California placer province: Tertiary
buried paleochannels later capped by lava flows, then re-exposed in the
Quaternary as the Yuba and American rivers incised through them. The
hydraulic-mining era (1850s-1884) opened Malakoff Diggins, Dutch Flat,
North San Juan, You Bet, Forest Hill, Iowa Hill, and Michigan Bluff.
Lindgren 1911 (USGS PP73, DOI 10.3133/pp73) remains the canonical
geological reference.

The v6 build is a two-population fusion. Tertiary deep-gravel positives
come from the Hydraulic Mine Pits of California (DOI 10.5066/F7J38QMD,
167 polygons); Quaternary modern-channel positives come from MRDS records
with placer dep_types. Each population trains its own classifier (PU
bagging baseline, Random Forest, LightGBM, stacking, isotonic calibration);
the calibrated rasters fuse via per-cell `np.maximum` into a single
deliverable GeoTIFF that the gldbg sibling repo
([johnsondevco.com/goldbug/](https://johnsondevco.com/goldbug/))
samples via its existing zonal-mean machinery. Phase 1 runs a USGS-Alaska-
style weighted knowledge-driven index first, with a validation gate that
every one of the seven anchor districts must land in the top decile
without being in the training labels; the gate has to pass before Phase 2
wires up.

The placer chapter coordinates with goldbug: goldbug did the SOTA survey,
corrected fabricated citations in the original SOTA writeup, and extended
the goldbug scanner to a new northern-Sierra region. ai-minerals builds
the model and ships the calibrated raster back. The handoff brief lives
at `research/placer_handoff_start_here.md` (and is the right entry point
for anyone resuming this work).

## Deeper notebooks

- [Eastern Alaska v1 integrated report](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/eastak/eastak_porphyry_prospectivity.html): full pipeline plus Kenorland blind test diagnostic.
- [BC Golden Triangle v2 integrated report](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/bcgt/bcgt_porphyry_prospectivity.html): 154-positive blind-drill validation against post-2015 holes.
- [Mother Lode v3 portfolio summary](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/prospectus/motherlode.html), [v3.1 improvements (8 experiments)](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/motherlode/v3p1_improvements.html), [v3 data exploration](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/motherlode/data_exploration.html).
- [Arizona v4 integrated report](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/arizona/main.html): Cox-Singer-cleaned 191-positive porphyry-Cu set with 4-cell DEEP-SEAM-style methodology comparison.
- [Northern Sierra placer v6 integrated report](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/northern_sierra_placer/northern_sierra_placer_prospectivity.html), [internal review notes](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/notebooks/northern_sierra_placer/internal.html): two-population PU + RF + LightGBM + stacking + isotonic, anchor-district validation, gldbg-handoff raster.

[← back to portfolio map](https://johnsondevco.com/ai-minerals/)
